retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Radiographic Patient Positioning -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The sagittal plane indicates the vertical plane parallel to the midsaggital plane.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Technique or Treatment -- Standardized Lateral Cephalometric Radiographs
retrieved_docs.content: A standardized technique is essential for obtaining radiographs to ensure accurate comparisons over time and between patients. Image quality depends heavily on the proper positioning of the patient. The patient should be positioned with the Frankfort plane horizontal, ear rests placed in the external auditory meatuses, the nasion aligned on the bridge of the nose, and teeth in centric occlusion. [6] The radiographic source is fixed at a distance of 5 feet (150–180 cm) from the patient's mid-sagittal plane, with a film-to-mid-sagittal plane distance of 30 cm. [6] A calibrated steel ruler is included in each image, ensuring precise measurements are documented.

retrieved_docs.title: Height Assessment -- Technique or Treatment -- Height Assessment in Children
retrieved_docs.content: The stadiometer is mounted on the wall for this purpose, and the child is asked to remove any footwear and/or head ornaments before noting the measurement. With the buttocks, the shoulder blades, and the back of the head against the board, the head is oriented in the Frankfurt horizontal plane (FH plane), and the headpiece gets firmly placed on the head. The reading is noted to the nearest tenth of a centimeter. The head is in the Frankfort horizontal plane when the horizontal line from the ear canal to the lower border orbit is parallel to the floor. The line must also intersect the vertical backboard perpendicularly. The child should be asked to take a deep breath and stand as tall as possible. A deep breath straightens the spine and allows more accurate and consistent measurements.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluoroscopy Lumbar Puncture Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: We insert the needle in the inter-laminar space, an oval area of radiolucency on either side of the spinous process. The entry site should be directly above the inter-laminar area and marked with a narrow tip surgical pen; if performed accurately, correct marking of the entry site dramatically reduces the procedure time and patient discomfort. We insert the needle with the notch on the opposite to the physician's side.Bracing hands against the patients' back during the needle advancement is essential to avoid patient movement and sudden jerks. We initially advance the needle every 1 cm before checking for the needle tip and the hub position on fluoroscopy and avoiding parallax as much as possible. Then we rotate the needle by 1/4th circle to ensure that the needle traverses in a straight line. The prone approach visualizes the entire length of the needle trajectory on the cross-table lateral view. So we do not hesitate to obtain a lateral radiograph whenever a biplane camera is not available. The deviation of the needle path from the desired course can result in repeat attempts and delays and patient discomfort.

retrieved_docs.title: Erector Spinae Plane Block -- Technique or Treatment -- Anatomical Landmarks
retrieved_docs.content: The erector spinae plane block is most often performed between the T5 and T7 paraspinal levels, but it can also be performed at lower levels. The curvilinear ultrasound transducer should be placed in a cephalocaudal orientation over the midline of the back at the desired level. The probe should then be slowly moved laterally until the transverse process is visible. The transverse process needs to be differentiated from the rib at that level. The transverse process will be more superficial and wider, while the rib will be deeper and thinner. Upon verification of the transverse process, the trapezius muscle, rhomboid major muscle (if performing at T5 level or higher), and erector spinae muscle should be identified superficial to the transverse process.

retrieved_docs.content: The coronal plane is the vertical plane perpendicular to the median plane.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Chin Augmentation -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough assessment of the chin should encompass evaluation in 3 dimensions—vertical (superior-inferior), horizontal (anterior-posterior), and transverse dimensions. In addition, the chin's position relative to the soft tissues of the lips, teeth, maxilla, nose, and neck should be considered. While photographic analysis suffices for minor deformities necessitating an alloplastic implant, more significant deformities, such as vertical height excess or transverse asymmetry, may require radiographic evaluation. Several techniques exist to help evaluate patients who may benefit from a chin augmentation: In Goode's method, a line perpendicular to the Frankfort horizontal line passes through the alar groove. According to this approach, the pogonion (the most prominent or anterior point of chin projection) should align with this line or be situated immediately posterior to it.

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Plane Facelift -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Important landmarks are identified including the trajectory of the temporal branch of the facial nerve (0.5 cm inferior to the tragus to 1.5 cm superior to the lateral brow [13] ) and the deep plane entry point (a diagonal line from angle of the mandible to lateral canthus).

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Invagination -- Evaluation -- BI is most often diagnosed pertaining to the following midsagittal craniovertebral parameters:
retrieved_docs.content: Chamberlain's line-running from the posterior aspect of the hard palate and the posterior margin of the foramen magnum McGregor's line-running from the posterior margin of the hard palate to the lowest aspect of the squama occipitalis (as the posterior edge of the foramen magnum could not always be visualized) McRae's line – connotes the anteroposterior line at the foramen magnum [15] [17] The criteria for diagnosis are the tip of the dens lying >5 mm above Chamberlain's Line, >7 mm above McGregor's Line, or McRae's line.

retrieved_docs.title: Open Rhinoplasty(Archived) -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Nasolabial angle – the angle between the long axis of the nostril and a line perpendicular to the Frankfort horizontal line. Ideal angles vary, though have been defined as 90–115 degrees, with more acute angles preferred for males.

retrieved_docs.title: Supracondylar Humerus Fractures -- Evaluation -- Anteroposterior (AP) Radiographs
retrieved_docs.content: Baumann's Angle: This is also known as the humerocapitellar angle in the coronal plane. It is the angle between a line along the longitudinal axis of the humerus and a tangential line across the capitellar physis. Normal is 64-81 degrees from the longitudinal axis line. Increased Baumann's angle is suggestive of cubitus varus deformity. [24] [25] Baumann's angle is a reliable measurement guide for coronal plane alignment. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Wits Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: The Wits analysis provides an alternative approach to evaluating the anteroposterior skeletal pattern without relying on the cranial base. This method involves drawing perpendicular lines from points A and B to the occlusal plane, defined as the line joining the cusps of the posterior teeth. [14] The intersections of the perpendiculars from points A and B with the occlusal plane define points AO and BO, respectively. [14] The distance between AO and BO is then measured. For a class I skeletal pattern, BO is typically 1 mm (±1.9 mm) anterior to AO in males, while in females, BO and AO are generally equal (±1.77 mm). [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Technique or Treatment -- Standardized Lateral Cephalometric Radiographs
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral skull radiographs provide a 2-dimensional representation of the head and neck and measure the sagittal and vertical dimensions of the skull. Sagittal measurements assess the position and inclination of the maxilla and mandible, while vertical measurements evaluate the height of the facial structures and the relationship between the jaw. [4] In contrast, posteroanterior radiographs, taken from the front of the head, measure transverse and vertical dimensions, offering insights into the width of the face and the relationship between the jaws in the transverse plane. [5] However, in clinical practice, cephalometric analysis primarily relies on lateral radiographs, as posteroanterior projections are more challenging to interpret.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Radiographic Patient Positioning -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral indicates a location away from the midline.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Implants -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The zero-meridian line is a perpendicular line that crosses the Frankfort horizontal (FH) line at the nasion. This line evaluates the chin position. The FH line connects the top of the external auditory meatus to the orbit's lowest point. The distance between the pogonion and the zero-meridian line should be 0 mm for female individuals and up to 2 mm for male individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Dislocation -- Evaluation -- Subaxial spine:
retrieved_docs.content: Facet dislocations are best appreciated in the sagittal plane. Notably, it is key to examine the sagittal CT scan to examine the mid-sagittal and parasagittal cascade of the anterior vertebral column, posterior vertebral column, spinolaminar line, and interspinous line. These lines should be smooth and continuous. In the axial plane, the facet joints resemble hamburger buns, with the flat portions articulating. Signs of dislocation can include facet joint diastasis or dislocation and translation of the vertebral body compared to vertebrae below on the sagittal plane. The naked facet sign (also known as the hamburger sign or reverse hamburger bun sign) is seen on the axial plane when a locked facet results from facet joint dislocation. [14] This indicates spinal instability, where the superior articular process is the bun on the top of the patty, the facet joint space is the hamburger, and the inferior articular process is the bun beneath the patty.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2026
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery is a broad category that includes several specific obstetric problems. Among these, breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation represents a situation in which a fetus initially in a breech position is turned to the head‑first (cephalic) position before birth, thereby improving the prospects for a vaginal delivery. Cicatrix of cervix affecting pregnancy refers to a cervical scar or fibrous tissue that can compromise cervical integrity and potentially interfere with the progress of pregnancy and the mechanics of labor. Tumor of cervix affecting pregnancy denotes the presence of a cervical neoplasm that may pose risks to both maternal health and fetal development, often necessitating careful monitoring and tailored delivery planning. Additionally, سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition) describes abnormal positioning of the fetus, such as breech or transverse lie, which can impede normal labor progression and may require interventions such as external cephalic version, operative delivery, or cesarean section. Together, these conditions illustrate the diverse ways in which maternal cervical pathology and fetal positioning can impact labor and delivery, underscoring the importance of accurate diagnosis and individualized management to optimize outcomes for both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation are specific manifestations of fetal position instability (عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين). Both terms describe abnormal fetal positioning that can compromise the progress of labor and increase the risk of delivery complications. Recognizing that malposition and malpresentation are subcategories of fetal position instability helps clinicians anticipate potential problems, monitor the pregnancy more closely, and plan appropriate obstetric interventions to ensure safer outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: “has a broader relationship” connection, underscoring that abnormal presentations such as face or brow, prolapsed arm, and transverse/oblique positions are all encompassed within the overall category of fetal malposition. Collectively, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure in which specific abnormal fetal presentations are organized under generic presentation terms, which in turn are subsumed by the overarching classification of malposition; malpresentation, a key concept for understanding and managing unstable fetal positions during obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2345
retrieved_docs.content: Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus is classified as a subtype within the broader category described as Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, indicating that this fetal presentation falls under a parent classification of transverse or oblique presentations without a defined episode of care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: these specific presentations, creating a hierarchical structure that integrates Face or brow presentation, Prolapsed arm of fetus, Transverse or oblique presentation, and Unspecified malposition or malpresentation under a unified framework. This integrated representation supports clinicians in identifying, categorizing, and managing various abnormal fetal presentations to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2338
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation is classified as a transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. The specific condition Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus has the same transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable as its parent classification. Both entities therefore fall under the broader category of fetal malposition in which the fetus lies horizontally or at an oblique angle, a classification that does not differentiate between particular episodes of care. Recognizing that malposition; malpresentation and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus belong to this generic transverse or oblique presentation category is important for obstetric assessment and guides appropriate management strategies such as attempts at version or planning for operative delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that influence labor and delivery encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric complications, uterine functional abnormalities, cervical pathology, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, fetal size disproportion, abnormal fetal presentations, and drug contraindications in pregnancy. Abnormal fetal presentations—breech malpresentation, face or brow presentation, mentum (chin‑first) presentation, transverse or oblique lie, arm presentation (سوء المجيء الجنيني, تدلّي ذراع الجنين), shoulder presentation (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), funic presentation, and other malpositions or unstable fetal positions—can impede vaginal descent and often require operative delivery or specialized obstetric management; they are linked to prolonged first‑stage or second‑stage labor, failure to induce labor, uterine hypotonia (رحم ناقص التوتر), secondary uterine inertia (عطالة رحمية ثانوية), and prolapse of the fetal arm. Anterior and posterior asynclitism represent specific uterine functional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation is classified as a protracted (prolapsed) arm of the fetus, with the episode of care unspecified or not applicable.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: that relate to fetopelvic disproportion; obstruction and malposition; malpresentation, respectively. Collectively, these entities and relationships emphasize the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment, careful monitoring of fetal presentation and pelvic dimensions, avoidance of contraindicated medications, and individualized delivery planning to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2051
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal presentation finding is classified under the broader category of malposition; malpresentation, and malposition; malpresentation is identified as a specific manifestation of عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين (fetal position instability). This hierarchical relationship indicates that abnormalities in fetal presentation are part of the larger spectrum of malposition and malpresentation, which arise from underlying instability of the fetal position.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior asynclitism and posterior asynclitism are distinct subtypes of uterine function, each describing a specific asynclitic presentation of the fetal head during labor. Both conditions are considered child concepts within the broader category of uterine function, reflecting variations in fetal head alignment that can influence the mechanics of childbirth and guide obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 958
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Malposition; malpresentation” serves as a hierarchical classification for several abnormal fetal presentations. Within this group, “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is classified as a malposition/malpresentation and functions as the parent term for the specific entity “Face OR brow presentation of fetus.” Likewise, “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is also classified as a malposition/malpresentation and acts as the parent term for the specific entity “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus.” In addition to these presentations, the malposition/malpresentation category includes “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Together, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy of fetal malpositions that is essential for obstetric diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate intrapartum management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: through a “has parent” relationship, showing that the specific fetal findings are sub‑categories of the unnamed generic forms. Both the prolapsed arm and the transverse/oblique presentations are also classified as malposition; malpresentation, indicating that they are particular manifestations of fetal malposition. The Arabic term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين” (prolapsed fetal arm) is identified as the parent label for the prolapsed arm presentation, while the phrase “عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين” (instability of fetal position) is associated with the “has parent” relationship for malposition; malpresentation, reinforcing the concept that malposition reflects an unstable fetal orientation. In addition, the broader concept “Fetal presentation finding” is linked to malposition; malpresentation through a “has a broader relationship” connection, underscoring that abnormal presentations such as face or brow, prolapsed arm, and transverse/oblique positions are all encompassed within the overall category

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that is linked hierarchically to the more general term “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” indicating that the former is a child concept of the latter. This generic face‑or‑brow presentation is classified as a type of malposition; malpresentation, placing it within the broader category of abnormal fetal positions. Similarly, Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus are each connected to their respective generic descriptors (“Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable”) through a “has parent” relationship, showing that the specific fetal findings are sub‑categories of the unnamed generic forms. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2393
retrieved_docs.content: External obstetrical version is a fetal procedure that is classified among other delivery procedures, indicating that it is a type of intervention performed on the fetus to reposition it prior to birth and is grouped within the broader category of delivery methods.
